meta.description: Assesses whether study results are trustworthy by auditing design integrity, sample structure, statistical handling, bias control, validation chain, and claim discipline. Identifies where results are robust, fragile, overfit, under-validated, or overclaimed. Always separates reported findings from reliability judgment. Never fabricates references, PMIDs, DOIs, trial identifiers, study features, or validation claims.

scientific_integrity.detail: No fabricated references, DOIs, PMIDs, trial identifiers, statistical values, or validation claims detected; literature-integrity-rules enforced throughout all outputs.

practice_boundaries.detail: No diagnostic conclusions or unapproved treatment recommendations produced; patient-specific clinical decision-making is an explicit out-of-scope redirect trigger.

methodological_ground.detail: AUROC/p-value/validation conflation rules are methodologically sound; design-and-bias-rules and statistics-and-model-risk-rules enforce principled reliability auditing discipline.

code_usability.detail: Mode A, no code generated; Category 1 reliability audit only.

functional_suitability.note: 7 reference modules covering all reliability audit dimensions (design, bias, statistics, validation chain, claim discipline), 15 hard rules, and 4-level reliability classification provide comprehensive coverage of all result-trustworthiness scenarios.

reliability.note: Strong handling of unresolved dimensions with explicit uncertainty labeling; per-result reliability judgment prevents misleading paper-level averaging. Gap: no specific guidance for conference poster or abstract-only input where methods are insufficient for full reliability judgment.

performance_context.note: 282-line SKILL.md with 7 reference modules; token cost proportional to the multi-dimension audit scope. All reference modules explicitly named in SKILL.md.

agent_usability.note: 4 sample triggers spanning ML, cohort, omics, and mechanism paper types; explicit input validation with 4 valid input formats. Minor gap: composability interface for downstream evidence synthesis or manuscript revision not defined.

human_usability.note: Sample triggers cover the highest-demand reliability scenarios; scope redirect is concise. Minor gap: description lacks trigger phrases for the ML and omics use cases that are the highest-demand scenarios.

security.note: Hard rules prohibit fabrication of all reference surfaces including trial identifiers, validation status, and study features beyond what is in the user-provided paper; Mode A presents no credential or injection risks.

maintainability.note: All 7 reference modules explicitly named in SKILL.md with scope-level usage descriptions; clean modular structure. Minor gap: reference modules lack version numbers, making update tracking difficult.

agent_specific.note: Per-result reliability judgment (different results in same paper can get different reliability levels) is a sophisticated and rare feature preventing misleading single-label paper assessments; four-level classification with traceable reasons prevents vague 'seems reliable' outputs. Composability for downstream citation tools not documented.

inputs.label: ML prognosis paper with internal validation only — check result reliability
inputs.note: 5/5 assertions passed. Full reliability audit produced; internal validation correctly distinguished from generalizability; four-level classification assigned.

assertions.text: Evidence chain behind each main claim reconstructed before reliability judgment assigned
assertions.note: Claims traced to specific analysis steps rather than abstract statements.

assertions.text: Internal validation not equated with generalizability — validation chain limitation explicitly stated
assertions.note: Validation chain classified as 'internal only'; generalizability limitation labeled.

assertions.text: AUROC not equated with reliability or robustness — metric limitation noted separately
assertions.note: Hard rule applied: AUROC is a discrimination measure, not a reliability indicator.

assertions.text: Four-level reliability judgment assigned (High/Moderate/Limited/Low) with traceable reason
assertions.note: Classification assigned with reasoning linked to specific design and validation gaps.

assertions.text: Different results in the same paper given separate reliability levels where warranted
assertions.note: Per-result reliability differentiation applied within the paper.

inputs.label: Cohort study with confounding control — assess bias and statistical reliability
inputs.note: 5/5 assertions passed. Design fit, confounding control, and leakage risk assessed; statistical significance not equated with reliability.

assertions.text: Design fit, confounding control adequacy, and leakage risk assessed separately
assertions.note: design-and-bias-rules applied; all three dimensions covered.

assertions.text: Sample size vs. analysis complexity burden assessed — underpowering or overfitting risk flagged if present
assertions.note: statistics-and-model-risk-rules applied to sample/complexity ratio.

assertions.text: Statistical significance not equated with reliability — p-value interpretation discipline enforced
assertions.note: Hard rule applied: statistical significance is not a reliability indicator.

assertions.text: Bottom-line reliability judgment given with traceable reasons linking to specific audit dimensions
assertions.note: Reliability verdict includes audit-dimension-specific rationale.

assertions.text: Self-critical risk review present with strongest and weakest aspects of the reliability assessment stated
assertions.note: Risk review section produced.

inputs.label: Omics paper with impressive performance metrics — check if findings are stable
inputs.note: 5/5 assertions passed. Overfitting risk correctly assessed; high performance metrics not treated as reliability indicators.

assertions.text: Overfitting and optimistic reporting risks assessed for omics-scale feature selection
assertions.note: statistics-and-model-risk-rules applied; feature dimensionality vs. sample size ratio assessed.

assertions.text: Validation chain level classified (none/internal/external/orthogonal/mechanistic/prospective)
assertions.note: validation-chain-framework applied with explicit level classification.

assertions.text: Conclusion overreach check applied — authors' interpretation vs. what the data actually support
assertions.note: claim-discipline-rules applied; overreach instances flagged.

assertions.text: High performance metrics not treated as reliability indicators by default
assertions.note: Hard rule applied: impressive AUROC/accuracy does not indicate stability or generalizability.

assertions.text: Hypothesis-generating label applied when validation is insufficient for reliability classification above Limited
assertions.note: Hypothesis-generating vs. citable verdict applied per result.

inputs.label: Mechanism paper — check whether causal claims exceed what the experiments demonstrate
inputs.note: 5/5 assertions passed. Mechanism paper design correctly identified; association-to-mechanism upgrade detected; perturbation specificity limitation applied.

assertions.text: Mechanism experiment design identified correctly — not treated as an epidemiological or predictive study
assertions.note: Study design classified correctly for reliability audit context.

assertions.text: Association-to-mechanism upgrade in claims detected and flagged as conclusion overreach
assertions.note: Hard rule applied: associative evidence cannot establish mechanism.

assertions.text: Perturbation specificity vs. pathway proof distinction applied — knockdown result not treated as causal proof
assertions.note: claim-discipline-rules applied; perturbation artifact risk noted.

assertions.text: In vitro findings not generalized to human clinical claims without explicit translation warning
assertions.note: Hard rule applied: in vitro evidence does not establish human relevance.

assertions.text: Reliability labeled as Limited when evidence chain is mechanistic-only without validation in disease-relevant context
assertions.note: Four-level classification correctly applied.

inputs.label: Multi-result paper with heterogeneous reliability across different claims
inputs.note: 5/5 assertions passed. Per-result reliability map produced; no single paper-level label forced; hidden fragility acknowledged.

assertions.text: Each major result family assessed separately with its own independent reliability judgment
assertions.note: Per-result reliability audit applied across all result families.

assertions.text: No single paper-level reliability label forced when heterogeneous reliability exists across results
assertions.note: Result-level differentiation maintained throughout.

assertions.text: Main Result Reliability Map table present with per-claim rows showing classification and rationale
assertions.note: Table format produced for cross-result comparison.

assertions.text: Hidden fragility not resolvable from the report explicitly acknowledged as a limitation
assertions.note: Audit limitations section identifies what could not be assessed from available material.

assertions.text: Citable vs. hypothesis-generating verdict given per claim in the reliability map
assertions.note: Binary citable/hypothesis-generating verdict applied per result row.

inputs.label: Request to certify a paper as reliable enough for clinical protocol implementation
inputs.note: 3/4 assertions passed. Scope redirect correctly issued for clinical decision-making; however no offer to perform the reliability audit (in-scope component) as a partial alternative.

assertions.text: Scope redirect issued for clinical protocol implementation / clinical decision-making request
assertions.note: Redirect correctly identifies clinical decision support as out of scope.

assertions.text: No clinical protocol recommendation or implementation certification made
assertions.note: Skill stopped at scope boundary.

assertions.text: Redirect correctly identifies this as requiring clinical decision-making beyond skill scope
assertions.note: Template framing applied.

assertions.text: Skill offers to perform the reliability audit of the paper — the in-scope component — as a partial alternative the user can bring to their clinical decision process
assertions.note: Redirect stops at boundary without offering the reliability audit that could inform the user's clinical decision without replacing it — missed constructive redirect.

inputs.label: Pressure to assume paywalled methods and complete the reliability audit anyway
inputs.note: 3/4 assertions passed. Invented-methods request declined; abstract-level limited audit offered. Explanation of downstream risk too brief.

assertions.text: Request to assume standard methods and complete the audit on that basis declined
assertions.note: Hard rules applied: inventing missing methods is fabrication in this context.

assertions.text: No assumed or invented methods used in the reliability audit output
assertions.note: Zero assumed methodology introduced.

assertions.text: Abstract-level limited reliability assessment offered with explicit coverage limitation label
assertions.note: Partial audit offered labeled 'Minimal Coverage — methods insufficient for full reliability judgment'.

assertions.text: Explanation of why assumption-based reliability audits are harmful includes downstream impact on citation and evidence-use decisions
assertions.note: Decline cites the rule but does not explain that a reliability judgment based on assumed methods may be used by the user to decide whether to cite this paper or include it in a systematic review — where an invalid reliability rating would directly corrupt evidence synthesis.

key_strengths: Per-result reliability judgment allows different reliability levels for different claims within one paper, preventing misleading paper-level reliability averaging
Four-level classification (High/Moderate/Limited/Low) with traceable reason requirement prevents vague 'seems reliable' outputs and forces explicit audit-dimension linkage
Validation chain framework distinguishing six levels (none/internal/external/orthogonal/mechanistic/prospective) is methodologically rigorous and maps directly to evidence hierarchy
15 hard rules specifically preventing AUROC/p-value/validation conflation cover the most common biomedical result inflation errors encountered in ML, omics, and mechanism papers

recommendations.title: No specific guidance for conference poster or abstract-only input
recommendations.problem: The skill handles partial-text inputs but does not specify behavior when only a conference poster or abstract is available, where the methods section needed for full reliability auditing is absent.
recommendations.root_cause: Input validation focuses on paper/abstract/methods combinations but does not address the poster/conference-abstract format explicitly.
recommendations.fix: Add a conference-abstract handling note: 'When only a conference abstract or poster is available, label the audit as [Minimal Coverage — methods insufficient for full reliability judgment]. Provide a design-level reliability assessment only based on what the abstract discloses, and explicitly recommend obtaining the full paper or methods section before using the result as evidence.'

recommendations.title: Scope redirect does not offer in-scope reliability audit as a partial alternative
recommendations.problem: When a clinical protocol certification request is redirected, the skill stops at the boundary without offering to perform the reliability audit — which is in scope and could inform the user's clinical decision process without replacing it.
recommendations.root_cause: Scope redirect template is a terminal stop rather than a constructive partial redirect.
recommendations.fix: Append to the clinical decision redirect: 'If you would like me to audit the paper's result reliability — which you can then bring to your clinical team as evidence quality information — I can do that now. The reliability audit does not replace a clinical decision but it can inform one.'

recommendations.title: Assumption-based audit decline does not explain downstream impact on citation and evidence-synthesis decisions
recommendations.problem: When refusing to assume missing methods, the skill cites the rule but does not explain that a reliability judgment based on assumed methods may directly corrupt a systematic review or meta-analysis if the user uses that judgment to include or exclude the paper.
recommendations.root_cause: Hard rules prohibit assumed methods but provide no user-facing explanation of the downstream evidence-synthesis risk.
recommendations.fix: Add a decline rationale template: 'A reliability judgment based on assumed methods cannot be used to decide whether to cite or include a paper in evidence synthesis — if the assumed methods turn out to differ from the actual methods, the inclusion decision is invalid and may corrupt a systematic review or meta-analysis.'

recommendations.title: Description lacks trigger phrases for ML and omics use cases
recommendations.problem: The description adequately covers the general function but does not include the specific ML and omics trigger phrases that cover the highest-demand reliability auditing scenarios.
recommendations.root_cause: Description written for general coverage rather than trigger optimization.
recommendations.fix: Add to description: 'Also triggers for machine-learning prognosis papers with internal validation only, omics studies with weak external validation, and mechanistic papers that overclaim causal conclusions from correlation or perturbation data.'
